title: Cancel One Thing This Week, with Kindness
subtitle: A three-minute activity for the over-promised week
excerpt: A three-minute activity for the week that has been over-promised. Pick one thing in the diary that should not be there. Send the message to cancel it kindly, before the resentment builds up.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The cancel-with-grace practice is a staple of writing on time management and on boundary work.

practice.whenToUse: Use at the start of a week that is already too full, or on a Sunday evening when the week ahead is starting to weigh on the body.

content.text: A three-minute activity for the week that has too much in it. Pick one thing in the diary that should not be there. Send the cancel message in the next ten minutes.
New to activity as a practice? Read
Most over-promised weeks have one thing in them that you said yes to and now regret. Sometimes two. The body has been carrying it since the yes.
Cancelling it now is a smaller move than living through the week and then being too tired to do the work that does matter.
Cancelling early is gentler than cancelling late. The other person has time to make a new plan. You have time to take the slot back for yourself or to leave it empty.
The body learns that the week is allowed to be edited. The yes is not a life sentence. Future yeses will be more carefully given.
Open the diary. Look at the week. Find the one thing your body sinks at.
Write the cancel message. Short. Kind. No long apology and no full explanation.
A workable script: "I'm so sorry, I'm not going to be able to make Thursday. I've over-committed. Can we reschedule for the new year or next month?"
Take the slot back. Either rest, or use the time for the work that mattered to you in the first place.
Original to homemade.education. The cancel-with-grace move is common in writing on time and on boundaries.
